g.data-narration: Welcome to Dijkstra's Algorithm - a fundamental greedy algorithm for finding shortest paths in weighted graphs.
g.text: university - BCS401 Analysis and Design of Algorithms - Module 4

g.data-narration: Dijkstra's Algorithm finds the shortest path from a source vertex to all other vertices in a weighted graph with non-negative weights.
g.text: A greedy algorithm that finds the shortest path from a source vertex to all other
vertices in a weighted graph. Named after Edsger W. Dijkstra (1956).
Key Requirement: All edge weights must be non-negative (w ≥ 0)

g.data-narration: This algorithm powers real-world applications like GPS navigation, network routing, and flight scheduling systems worldwide.
g.text: Given: Weighted graph G=(V,E), source s
Find: Shortest distance from s to all v∈V

g.data-narration: The problem asks to find shortest paths from source vertex S to all vertices, assuming non-negative edge weights throughout.
g.text: Given a weighted graph G = (V, E) with source vertex s:
• Objective: Find shortest path distance from s to every vertex v ∈ V
• Constraint: All edge weights must be non-negative (w(u,v) ≥ 0)

g.data-narration: Three main data structures track distances, visited vertices, and the priority queue during algorithm execution.
The algorithm initializes distances, then repeatedly extracts the minimum vertex and relaxes its neighbors to find shorter paths.
g.text: 1. Initialize: dist[s] = 0, dist[v] = ∞ for all v ≠ s. Insert all vertices into priority queue.
• Extract vertex u with minimum distance from queue
If dist[u] + w(u,v) < dist[v], then dist[v] = dist[u] + w(u,v)

g.data-narration: Let's trace Dijkstra's algorithm on this graph with vertices A through E, starting from source vertex A.
g.text: Graph: {A,B,C,D,E} with edges: A-B(4), A-C(3), B-C(1), B-D(2), B-E(2), C-D(5), D-E(3)

g.data-narration: Initially all distances are infinite except source A. We extract A first, then update neighbors B and C with new distances.
g.text: Initial: dist[A]=0, dist[B]=∞, dist[C]=∞, dist[D]=∞, dist[E]=∞
Extract C (dist=3): No improvement (D stays ∞ from C path: 3+5=8 > ∞)

g.data-narration: After extracting B with distance 4, we update D and E. Then D is extracted with distance 6, improving E's distance.
g.text: Extract B (dist=4): Update D→6 (4+2), E→6 (4+2), C stays 3 (4+1=5 > 3)
Extract D (dist=6): Update E→9 (6+3), but keep E=6 (better path via B)
Extract E (dist=6): No further updates needed

g.data-narration: The algorithm's correctness relies on a key invariant: once a vertex is extracted, its shortest distance is permanently finalized.
g.text: Key Invariant: When vertex u is extracted from priority queue, dist[u] = shortest distance from source.
Why? All remaining vertices have distance ≥ dist[u] (min-heap property). Any alternative path to u must
go through an unvisited vertex with distance ≥ dist[u]. Therefore, no shorter path to u exists!

g.data-narration: Time complexity varies by data structure: O(V²) with arrays, O((V+E)logV) with binary heaps, and O(E+VlogV) with Fibonacci heaps.
Dijkstra's algorithm is faster but only works with non-negative weights, while Bellman-Ford handles negative weights at O(VE) cost.
g.text: O(V²) or O(E log V) • Non-negative weights only
O(VE) • Handles negative weights • Detects negative cycles

g.data-narration: Remember these essential formulas: relaxation condition, time complexities, and space complexity for complete understanding.
g.text: if dist[u] + w(u,v) < dist[v], then dist[v] = dist[u] + w(u,v)
O(V + E) for graph + O(V) for aux structures

g.data-narration: Avoid these common mistakes: forgetting source initialization, applying to negative weights, and confusing visited sets.
g.text: ✗ Forgetting to initialize dist[source] = 0 (causes all distances to be ∞)
✗ Applying Dijkstra when negative edge weights exist (algorithm will fail)
✗ Not checking for better paths before updating distances during relaxation

g.data-narration: For exams: practice step-by-step tables, memorize time complexities, and compare with Bellman-Ford. Good luck!
g.text: • Practice maintaining step-by-step tables with dist[] array at each iteration
• Remember: O(V²) for array, O((V+E)logV) for heap • Know when to use each implementation
Solve previous year university questions for better preparation!
